title: Pod Security, Security Contexts, Images, Admission, Secrets, and Multi-Tenancy · Reconciliation Brief: K8s Pod Security Standards Admission
summary: A hostile document workload requests privileged host access, mutable images, broad credentials, unrestricted egress, and co-location with sensitive tenants. Planned artifact: Add and verify the k8s pod security standards admission increment in restricted workload and admission policy. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Apply Privileged, Baseline, and Restricted Pod Security Standards with enforce, audit, warn, version pinning, exemptions, namespace labels, and rollout evidence.
Use TokenRequest and projected bound tokens with audience, expiry, rotation, revocation, automount controls, authorization checks, and periodic review.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

starterFiles.config:
# kubernetes-basics cumulative orchestration portfolio
# Browser labs perform deterministic manifest and evidence review only.
# They never invoke kubectl, read kubeconfig, contact a cluster API, or execute host commands.
workspace: kubernetes
version-baseline:
  kubernetes: 1.36.2
  minikube: 1.38.1
  gateway-api: 1.5.1
  helm: 4.2.2
transfer-boundary: authorized-disposable-cluster
evidence:

steps.title: Predict K8s Pod Security Standards Admission · Theory K8s Pod Security Standards Admission
steps.instruction:
theory case — A theory Pod runs as root, is privileged, adds capabilities, mounts hostPath, permits privilege escalation, and bypasses a namespace policy exception. Enforce Pod Security, securityContext, seccomp, admission, exception ownership, and residual shared-kernel risk.

content.title: Reasoning move
content.text: Name context, namespace, API identity, desired spec, observed status, controller authority, workload, network, storage, identity, policy, and failure-domain boundaries; predict reconciliation; review the smallest deterministic manifest change; then verify a failure, repair, rollback, and disposable-cluster transfer limit. Apply that move specifically to K8s Pod Security Standards Admission while producing restricted workload and admission policy.

content.title: Worked case pattern
content.text: A worked example Pod runs as root, is privileged, adds capabilities, mounts hostPath, permits privilege escalation, and bypasses a namespace policy exception. Enforce Pod Security, securityContext, seccomp, admission, exception ownership, and residual shared-kernel risk. Competency probe: Apply Privileged, Baseline, and Restricted Pod Security Standards with enforce, audit, warn, version pinning, exemptions, namespace labels, and rollout evidence. A defensible solution applies K8s Pod Security Standards Admission, preserves the governing invariant or value contract, and connects the observed result to restricted workload and admission policy.

content.code:
# Worked focus: k8s-pod-security-standards-admission — Apply Privileged, Baseline, and Restricted Pod Security Standards with enforce, audit, warn, version pinning, exemptions, namespace labels, and rollout evidence.
  # Evidence: k8s-worked-k8s-workload-security-admission-4
  - competency: k8s-worked-k8s-workload-security-admission-4
    artifact: restricted-workload-policy.yaml
    decision: worked k8s workload security admission 4
    environment: air-gapped release cluster using an internal registry mirror
    fault-injection: drop one metrics series during an autoscaling surge
    retained-proof: accessible incident timeline with causal regression evidence
    case-key: worked4
    change: |
      pod-security: restricted-v1.36
      runAsNonRoot: true
      allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
      capabilities-drop: ALL
      seccompProfile: RuntimeDefault
      readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
      image: digest-and-signature-required
      admission: fail-closed-with-breakglass
    verify: kubectl auth can-i create pods -n intake && kubectl apply --server-side --dry-run=server -f restricted.yaml
    failure: Reject removed PodSecurityPolicy, namespace-only multi-tenancy, mutable images, privileged host access, broad secrets, or unavailable admission without a plan.
    repair: Enforce versioned Pod Security, minimize container and API authority, isolate network and storage, verify supply chain, and test break-glass recovery.
content.caption: Runnable Kubernetes object and transfer-evidence contract example; predict its result before running, then change one boundary input.

content.title: Non-worked case
content.text: The removed PodSecurityPolicy API is the current built-in way to enforce Restricted workloads. This fails the K8s Pod Security Standards Admission contract because it omits a precondition, invariant, ownership rule, or counterexample needed by this exact case.
